feedback.text: Hello there, I have a suggestion for an addition to this act, which I think would be an important part of consumer protections. Currently, there's a trend in the industry of videogames where games require online verification to be played, as a part of digital rights management. Sometimes this is part of online multiplayer functionality, but not always. - - - - - - - - - This isn't new in itself, but what has changed over the years is that once a developer or publisher of such a game decide it's no longer profitable to keep their game up, they shut down the servers. Naturally, they can't be expected to host an unprofitable venture out of good will, but this creates a problem where said game will then just disappear from the world. You paid for this videogame, but now you can't play it ever again. This did not use to be the case, 30 years ago you could buy a game for a computer or a console, and it was yours forever, you would just play it whenever you wanted, just like if you bought a home copy of a movie. Now, imagine if you bought a Blu-Ray copy of the movie 1975 Steven Spielberg movie Jaws (that's a really good one), but it required you to connect to the internet to be able to watch the movie, and now imagine that Universal Pictures decided that they didn't want to bother with verification anymore and they shut down their service for that. Now you couldn't watch that copy of Jaws anymore, which you paid 89.99 for, that wasn't cheap, but it doesn't stop there, imagine that Universal decided that they would never publish Jaws again either, sorry, you'll have to settle for Jaws 2, and it's the same song and dance, and eventually they destroy that one too, you can never watch Jaws or Jaws 2 again, but hey, at least they're putting out Jaws 3 and, uh, oh boy, Jaws 4. D: Perhaps you really liked the first movie, and you think it's a really well acted suspense thriller which has significant cultural value, but sorry, you can't have it anymore. Perhaps you really like Jaws 4 for some strange reason, but that one is just the same due for the chopping block, they don't even want you to keep a BAD movie. - - - - - - - - - This is essentially what has been happening increasingly with videogames in the past 15 years or so. Mind, this isn't framed as a rental service, but rather a one-time purchase, and it's never disclosed WHEN support ends, it's just whenever they decide it's not worth their time anymore, and you're left with NO recourse, the game is just gone forever. Some in the industry are insistent that it's impossible to realistically address this, but that's far from the truth, and it would not be an undue logistical burden at all, nor would addressing this have to require the rights holders to surrender intellectual property. I really don't think it's fair to frame this as a purchase, nor to delete the product wholly after a completely unclear and indeterminate time frame, it's a destruction of art and culture as well. HUNDREDS of games have been lost this way, and hundreds more are at risk. - - - - - - - - - I have personal experience with this too. Back when the classic videogame Super Mario Bros. had its 35th anniversary, so Nintendo made a special version of it where large numbers of people would compete against each other online in a sort of 'Last Man Standing' premise where you could send extra enemies to the other players, and they could do the same to you, and so it was a quick and frantic competition to see who would be left, it got REALLY hectic and wild, and it looked exciting and fun. I considered buying a Switch just for that game, but then I learned that the game was going to be closed forever after a month. Sure, I had my opportunity to try to play it, but I would never get to see it again after that, just because Nintendo decided I shouldn't. Please think about it, the Stop Destroying Games citizen's initiative already has over 1.4 Million signatures in the hopes of addressing this.
